title: Revisiting the Dunning-Kruger effect: Composite measures and heterogeneity by gender
journal: Journal of Behavioral and Experimental Economics
abstract: • We test for the Dunning-Kruger effect using composite measures and birth cohort data. • We use tests from ages 5/10/16 and self-estimates of ability at ages 10/16. • We show that the Dunning-Kruger effect exists in our secondary data. • We find that the Dunning-Kruger effect is not gender specific. • Women are underconfident and men are overconfident. The Dunning-Kruger effect (DKE) states that people with lower levels of the ability tend to self-assess their ability less accurately than people with relatively higher levels of the ability. Thus, the correlation between one's objective cognitive abilities and self-assessed abilities is higher at higher levels of objective cognitive abilities. There has been much debate as to whether this effect actually exists or is a statistical artefact. This paper replicates and extends Gignac and Zajenkowski (2020) and Dunkel, Nedelec, and van der Linden (2023) to test whether the DKE exists using several measures of ability and nationally representative data from a British birth cohort study. To do this, we construct a measure of objective cognitive abilities using 18 tests conducted at ages 5, 10, and 16, and a measure of subjective self-assessed abilities using estimates of school performance and being clever at ages 10 and 16. We replicate their models and show that the DKE exists in our secondary data. Importantly, we are the first to look at whether this relationship is heterogeneous by gender and find that while the self-assessment bias is gender specific, the DKE is not. The DKE comes from men relatively overestimating and women relatively underestimating their abilities.
summary_en: The Dunning-Kruger effect — that less-skilled people overestimate their ability more — has been questioned as a statistical artifact. This paper uses UK longitudinal cohort data with ability tests at ages 5, 10, and 16 plus self-estimates at 10 and 16, constructing composite measures robust to previous critiques. The effect is real. But the gender story is nuanced: the Dunning-Kruger pattern itself is not gender-specific, yet women are systematically underconfident and men overconfident across ability levels. A careful empirical vindication with a more nuanced gender interpretation.
data_used: UK longitudinal cohort data (likely BCS70/NCDS) with ability tests at multiple ages (5, 10, 16) and subjective ability self-assessments at ages 10 and 16. Composite-measure approach to mitigate regression-to-mean critiques.
policy_relevance: Relevant to Hungarian education-policy and gender-equality interventions. The paper shows confidence-building for women needs to be targeted at all ability levels, not just low-performers. Relevant to STEM education, school-career-counselling design, and workplace-promotion practices. Caveat: UK cohort; Hungarian equivalent would require KRTK-linked longitudinal data.
